result.chapter_summary: Literacy in the public sphere requires more than decoding; it necessitates a foundation of common knowledge and standard language conventions that have been neglected due to 'naturalistic' educational theories. The abandonment of systematic phonics and grammar instruction has disadvantaged students and paralyzed teachers, leading to a decline in writing competence that persists into higher education.

claims.text: Primers and spelling books have historically been understood to have a larger goal than just imparting the secrets of the written code.
claims.source_passage: Primers and spelling books have always been understood to have a larger goal than just imparting the secrets of the written code.

claims.text: Primers and spelling books are intended to impart common knowledge and values that accompany language use in the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: They are also meant to impart some of the common knowledge and values that accompany language use in the public sphere.

claims.text: Schools are responsible for teaching children the underlying assumptions that accompany the use of the standard written and oral code.
claims.source_passage: The schools have been responsible for teaching children not only the standard written and oral code but also some of the underlying assumptions that go with its use.

claims.text: A primary goal of the common school is to teach the common language so that all citizens can participate in the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: A goal of the common school is to teach the common language so that all can participate in the public sphere.

claims.text: The discrepancy between the language spoken at home and the standard print code has led to charges of language imperialism.
claims.source_passage: But for other children it is not, and this has led to resentful charges of language imperialism.

claims.text: American schools have been paralyzed by controversies regarding the print code, leading to a laissez-faire approach to instruction.
claims.source_passage: American schools have thus been caught up in controversies that have paralyzed textbook writers, teachers, and administrators even beyond the laissez-faire approach to the print code recommended by some in the anti-curriculum movement.

claims.text: Teaching 'Ebonics' to African-American students in school is a terrible idea.
claims.source_passage: He simply said, “It’s a terrible idea.” Among educators, only Al had the courage to be so blunt. I will try to explain why he was right

claims.text: The 'natural approach' to reading theory claims that immersion in authentic language leads to more successful decoding than the study of phonics.
claims.source_passage: A natural approach, the theory said, will unerringly lead to the right result.

claims.text: The whole-language method fails to teach the decoding of letters into language sounds when it neglects systematic phonics.
claims.source_passage: to the extent that it neglects systematic phonics, it still fails to teach the decoding of letters into language sounds.

claims.text: The whole-language movement has gone 'underground' by rebranding itself as 'balanced literacy.'
claims.source_passage: Teachers now say that the recent science has simply driven the whole-language movement underground. It has adopted a new label, “balanced literacy”

claims.text: Most state standards in language arts do not require early grade children to learn the parts of speech.
claims.source_passage: Today, most state standards in language arts do not require children to learn the parts of speech in the early grades.

claims.text: Prospective teachers are incorrectly told that linguistics has proved the inadvisability of teaching correct pronunciation and grammar.
claims.source_passage: Prospective teachers are told in authoritative tones that linguistics has proved the inadvisability of teaching correct pronunciation and grammar.

claims.text: Essential information regarding Standard American Written English is not being transmitted to the elementary schoolteachers who need it most.
claims.source_passage: This information is not being transmitted to the elementary schoolteachers who need it most.

claims.text: The lack of clearly defined curriculum expectations regarding grammar adds significant work and stress to teachers' lives.
claims.source_passage: Besides putting students at a great disadvantage, particularly those who transfer classes or schools mid-year, the lack of clearly defined expectations adds an enormous amount of work and stress to my life.

claims.text: Student writing in college has become so poor that it discourages faculty from attempting to improve it.
claims.source_passage: Student writing has become so bad that it defeats the impulse to improve it.

claims.text: College faculty members increasingly utilize short-answer and multiple-choice tests specifically to avoid the burden of reading poorly written student papers.
claims.source_passage: Short-answer and multiple-choice tests have become the preferred evaluation method, so that faculty members can avoid having to read student papers.

claims.text: Student writing in higher education has reached a level of poor quality that discourages attempts at improvement.
claims.source_passage: Student writing has become so bad that it defeats the impulse to improve it.

claims.text: Remedial writing students lose the skills they acquire in remediation if those skills are not reinforced through use in other courses.
claims.source_passage: Al Fiellin... discovered that remedial writing students were losing through disuse whatever skills they had acquired in remediation.

claims.text: The resolution 'Students’ Right to Their Own Language' is factually inadmissible and ethically suspect.
claims.source_passage: The rest is simply incorrect, and I am embarrassed that two organizations I once belonged to are still supporting this factually inadmissible and ethically suspect resolution.

claims.text: Standard American English is a concrete and stable reality of American life, not a myth.
claims.source_passage: Mainstream linguistic scholarship recognizes that standard American English is no myth. It is the language of your local newspaper, radio station, and favorite blog. The standard language is one of the firmest and most stable realities of American life.

claims.text: Standard American English serves as a primary tool for economic competence, social equality, and national solidarity.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, the standard language is the great organ of economic competence, social equality, and solidarity.

claims.text: To restrict a student's mastery of the dominant dialect is to deny them access to social and political power.
claims.source_passage: Even if one argues (incorrectly) that there is no standard American dialect but only a dialect of “power,” to restrict students’ mastery of that dialect is to deny them access to power

claims.text: Failing to teach Standard English explicitly denies children the ability to participate fully in civic activity and express grievances effectively.
claims.source_passage: By refusing to teach it explicitly, we deny children a vital tool that they need to participate fully in civic activity, make their voices heard, and make their grievances felt.

claims.text: Inability to communicate in the standard dialect leads to harsh social judgments regarding an individual's intelligence and abilities.
claims.source_passage: We limit their job prospects and economic viability and sentence them to a life of harsh judgments from members of the broader speech community about their intelligence and abilities, based solely on their inability to communicate in the standard dialect.

claims.text: The failure to provide explicit instruction in the conventions of standard language and its accompanying knowledge is an abdication of a teacher's duty.
claims.source_passage: If such explicitness is not provided to students, what it feels like to people who are old enough to judge is that there are secrets being kept, that time is being wasted, that the teacher is abdicating his or her duty to teach.

claims.text: A shared standard language is necessary in the public sphere for democratic communication and decision making, regardless of what dialect is spoken in private.
claims.source_passage: But whatever language Americans speak in the private sphere, they need to use Standard English in the public sphere, not just for practical economic purposes but also to facilitate communication and decision making in a democracy.

claims.text: The American Founders preferred a common language to support ideals of civility, mutual respect, and goodwill.
claims.source_passage: Gordon S. Wood reminds us that the American Founders preferred a common language not just for practical reasons but also to support ideals of civility, mutual respect, and goodwill

claims.text: Early dictionary compilers sought to eliminate peculiarities of dialect and eccentricities of spelling to set standards for the language.
claims.source_passage: Compilers of dictionaries attempted to find the correct meanings, spellings, and punctuations of words, and freeze them between the covers of their books. In these ways peculiarities of dialect, and eccentricities of spelling and pronunciation could be eliminated, and standards of the language could be set

claims.text: The founding-era concept of a 'gentleman' is equivalent to the modern idea of a liberal arts education.
claims.source_passage: [Being a gentleman] signified being cosmopolitan...It meant, in short, having all those characteristics that we today sum up in the idea of a liberal arts education.

claims.text: Present-day academic theorists argue that universalist ideals of civility and a common language are actually the prejudices of the white, Anglo-Saxon bourgeoisie.
claims.source_passage: The supposedly universalist ideals of civility and a common language are really the history-bound prejudices of a dominant class—the white, Anglo-Saxon bourgeoisie who seek to impose their values and language on those who have different sets of values or different dialects.

claims.text: Arguments for 'students’ right to their own language' are self-defeating because they must be expressed in Standard English to be taken seriously in the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: If the argument for non-standard language were made in nonstandard language, no one would pay serious attention. The assertion of students’ right to their own language therefore amounts to this: “You have no right to insist that American students be taught to write or speak in the only way that other Americans will pay serious attention to.”

claims.text: The standard American language is not a class dialect, but an artificial construct that is the property of all citizens.
claims.source_passage: The standard American language is not a class dialect, nor is the standard knowledge that underlies it the property of a subgroup. The standard language is an artificial construct, created over centuries... Both Standard English and the knowledge that enables its use are the property of all.

claims.text: Universal mastery of Standard English and its coordinate knowledge is essential for real democracy.
claims.source_passage: Universal mastery of them is essential to real democracy. Only those who master that language and its coordinate knowledge are full participants in the linguistic commons and the public sphere.

claims.text: Attributing the current character of a language solely to its historical origins (such as royal courts) is a 'genetic fallacy.'
claims.source_passage: But to pay too much attention to dialectal origins is to commit the genetic fallacy—the assumption that the genesis of a thing limits and defines its later character.

claims.text: The United States did not develop its own standard dialect but inherited an already standardized language from England.
claims.source_passage: Whose dialect did become the American language? Nobody’s. By the time the United States was forming into a nation, we had inherited this artificial construct from the language standardizers of England.

claims.text: When schools teach standardized syntax, grammar, spelling, and pronunciation, the print code eventually becomes the spoken language of the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: t all children attend schools where they are taught the “correct” forms of syntax, grammar, spelling, and pronunciation, the print code becomes, after some generations, the language spoken in the public sphere, and often at the mother’s knee.

claims.text: The grammatical structure of the English language has remained largely unchanged since the late nineteenth century.
claims.source_passage: ever since the late nineteenth century, new words have occupied a surprisingly small segment of the English vocabulary, and grammar has hardly changed at all.

claims.text: Standardized languages are highly resistant to change once established.
claims.source_passage: The standard language then becomes very resistant to change. This stability is a key point that gets glossed over in the language popularizations teachers normally see.

claims.text: New vocabulary additions occupy a very small segment of the total English vocabulary compared to the stable core.
claims.source_passage: Such vocabulary change is a fascinating subject, but ever since the late nineteenth century, new words have occupied a surprisingly small segment of the English vocabulary, and grammar has hardly changed at all.

claims.text: Dictionaries remain reliable guides to language for a century or more due to the conservatism of standardized forms.
claims.source_passage: there is good reason to believe that in 2034, when my current one is a hundred years old, it will still be a reliable guide to the main meanings of its three hundred thousand words, and their spellings and pronunciations.

claims.text: The stability of language is reinforced by the accumulation of printed media in libraries.
claims.source_passage: It is reinforced not just by growing use but also by all the books and magazines and blogs that come out each day and collect in the library.

claims.text: Language stability originated from the printing press's role in disseminating the written word to the public.
claims.source_passage: Such stability, still relatively new in the history of language, owes its origin to the standardizing role of the printing press in disseminating the written word to increasingly large segments of the public.

claims.text: Before language standardization, English changed so rapidly that writers became unintelligible within sixty years.
claims.source_passage: In the early eighteenth century, just before language standardization became dominant in England, Alexander Pope worried that he would not be understood after sixty years because of what language change had done to English writers before him.

claims.text: Language standardization allows modern students to read 18th-century texts that would have been unintelligible in previous eras.
claims.source_passage: But because Pope was lucky enough to flourish at the start of the era of language standardization, American schoolchildren of today can read these very lines.

claims.text: The United States was founded in a period where the English language had already achieved a stable, standardized form.
claims.source_passage: He might have said “later than the eighteenth century,” the era when our Republic arose fully formed in a linguistic sense. Our founding documents, the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution, are written and spelled in essentially our own language.

claims.text: Language is inherently mutable and prone to rapid change whenever it exists only in oral form.
claims.source_passage: Mutability had always been its fate, and it still is wherever the language exists only in oral form.

claims.text: Before mass literacy, local dialects were so distinct they were mutually unintelligible across short distances.
claims.source_passage: Dialects a hundred miles apart were mutually unintelligible.

claims.text: The development of modern nations required the expansion of literacy and communication to include citizens far from political centers.
claims.source_passage: The newly developing nations depended for their effective functioning on an expansion of literacy and communication among all citizens, no matter how far they might be from the centers of political and economic power.

claims.text: The establishment of national schools to teach a uniform national tongue was a novel development in human history.
claims.source_passage: Such imposition of language uniformity over big geographical stretches, central to the modern world, was something new in human experience.

claims.text: The economic and political existence of the modern nation is impossible without language standardization spread via universal schooling.
claims.source_passage: Without language standardization spread by universal schooling, the economic and political network that is the modern nation could not exist.

claims.text: The modern industrial nation depends for its effective functioning on an expansion of literacy and communication among all citizens, regardless of their proximity to power centers.
claims.source_passage: The newly developing nations depended for their effective functioning on an expansion of literacy and communication among all citizens, no matter how far they might be from the centers of political and economic power.

claims.text: American schools have a duty to make every child conversant with the spoken and written forms of Standard English.
claims.source_passage: In sum, since the beginning of the United States in the late eighteenth century, schools have had a duty to make every child conversant with the spoken and written forms of Standard English.

claims.text: The grammatical forms and spellings of Standard English have remained stable since the founding of the United States and transcend social class.
claims.source_passage: The forms themselves have remained stable since the founding, and they transcend class: schools have had the duty to bring them to all classes of society.

claims.text: Competency in Standard English is a prerequisite for reaching the top tier of society.
claims.source_passage: Children who have not been properly taught the stable, standard forms can never belong to the top tier of society.

claims.text: The association between Standard English and social class is contingent rather than inherent; command of the language is a prerequisite for wealth, not just a byproduct of it.
claims.source_passage: But is this because well-off people belong to the class that deploys Standard English, or is it because deploying Standard English is increasingly a prerequisite to being well-off ? That the latter is the more likely explanation is suggested by listening to army generals.

claims.text: The primary difference between American and British speech communities lies in unspoken, tacit knowledge rather than grammar or vocabulary.
claims.source_passage: While George Bernard Shaw’s wisecrack about the two nations being separated by a common language has an element of truth, it has almost nothing to do with grammar and spelling and everything to do with tacit knowledge. The assumed knowledge of the British public sphere is different from that of the American public sphere.

claims.text: Communication in reading and writing takes place within a context of shared, unspoken knowledge and values.
claims.source_passage: Communication is at the center of reading and writing, and it takes place in a context of shared, unspoken knowledge and values.

claims.text: To enable all children to communicate, schools must explicitly teach the unspoken knowledge that is taken for granted in writing addressed to the general public.
claims.source_passage: On the contrary, the unspoken knowledge that is assumed in writing addressed to the general public must be made explicit and imparted to all.

claims.text: American schools have successfully begun to implement the systematic teaching of the mechanics of literacy, such as phonics and phonemic awareness.
claims.source_passage: Thanks to the scientific work sponsored by the National Institutes of Health and publicized by the National Reading Panel, the systematic teaching of alphabetic reading and writing is a frontier that our schools have begun to cross.

claims.text: Phonics instruction improves reading scores in early grades where mechanics are tested, but it does not improve scores in later grades where comprehension is the focus.
claims.source_passage: But while the teaching of phonics has our children advancing in reading scores in the early grades, where the mechanics of reading are the main things probed on reading tests, they are not advancing in the later grades, when the tests’ main focus is language comprehension.

claims.text: Despite massive reforms and efforts like No Child Left Behind, K-12 students cannot read or communicate effectively because schools fail to impart the background knowledge required for language use.
claims.source_passage: That is because the schools are not effectively imparting the knowledge that the effective use of standard language depends on. This knowledge has to be just as deliberately transmitted by the schools as phonemic awareness and blending.

claims.text: The obligation of schools to teach reading and writing necessitates teaching students how to communicate with an unknown and unseen audience.
claims.source_passage: If schools have an obligation to teach reading and writing, it follows that they must also teach students how to communicate with an unknown and unseen audience.

claims.text: Schools are not effectively imparting the knowledge that the effective use of standard language depends on.
claims.source_passage: That is because the schools are not effectively imparting the knowledge that the effective use of standard language depends on.

claims.text: Standardized background knowledge must be just as deliberately transmitted by schools as phonemic awareness and blending.
claims.source_passage: This knowledge has to be just as deliberately transmitted by the schools as phonemic awareness and blending.

claims.text: Communication across wide distances (e.g., email and the Internet) makes predicting the knowledge of an unknown audience a key task of modern literacy.
claims.source_passage: For an author, predicting what an unknown, unseen audience can be expected to know is a key task of writing, whether in a newspaper or a fourth-grade classroom exercise.

claims.text: A dimension of unstated knowledge is a universal characteristic of all human language.
claims.source_passage: A dimension of unstated knowledge is a universal characteristic of language.

claims.text: Efficient written communication requires a significant amount of shared relevant knowledge between the writer and the reader.
claims.source_passage: To make written communication reasonably efficient, a good deal of relevant knowledge must already be shared between interlocutors.

claims.text: The public dimension of shared knowledge is implicitly standardized in the same way as other linguistic conventions.
claims.source_passage: Those who successfully communicate to a wide audience are able to do so because the public dimension of shared knowledge is, at any given time, implicitly standardized like the other conventions of language.

claims.text: Belonging to the specific speech community of the United States requires basic knowledge of culturally specific topics like baseball.
claims.source_passage: To belong to the speech community of the United States today you need some basic knowledge of, say, baseball.

claims.text: Comprehending irony depends primarily on unspoken knowledge and shared attitudes rather than innate humor or intellectual subtlety.
claims.source_passage: All irony depends on unspoken knowledge and attitudes shared between speaker and listener, between writer and reader

claims.text: Irony is a common feature of everyday language in both rural and urban settings, not an elite phenomenon.
claims.source_passage: But it is definitely not an elite phenomenon. Rural and city talk are full of irony.

claims.text: Equality in the public sphere requires that all participants share a vast amount of unspoken background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: To be members of the wider club and to stand as equals on the linguistic commons of the nation, the participants in the public sphere have to share a great deal of unspoken background knowledge.

claims.text: The view that reading comprehension is a formal ability improvable through strategy drills on 'whimsical fictions' is a tragic educational mistake.
claims.source_passage: The notion that reading comprehension is some sort of formal ability that can be learned by practicing strategy drills on whimsical fictions is a tragic mistake.

claims.text: Commonality of language requires commonality of knowledge to enable communication in the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: in order to enable communication in the public sphere, commonality of language requires commonality of knowledge.

claims.text: Schooling takes many years primarily because of the extensive nature of the unstated knowledge required for literacy.
claims.source_passage: Indeed, the vastness of this knowledge is the chief reason schooling takes so many years. If reading and writing were simply how-to skills, three or four years would suffice.

claims.text: Effective communication requires a writer to be able to predict the knowledge already possessed by their audience.
claims.source_passage: If I as a writer cannot predict what my unseen audience already knows, then I cannot know how to communicate with it.

claims.text: The core school curriculum should prioritize the most durable and least ephemeral tacit public knowledge.
claims.source_passage: In deciding on the core contents of the school curriculum, it makes sense, therefore, to place stress on the most durable, least ephemeral tacit public knowledge.

claims.text: Reading comprehension scores at schools following the Core Knowledge curriculum have increased significantly.
claims.source_passage: Unsurprisingly, reading comprehension scores at these schools have soared.

claims.text: The lack of productivity in American classrooms is primarily caused by diversity of academic preparation rather than diversity of ethnic or family background.
claims.source_passage: the American classroom’s lack of productivity is not chiefly caused by diversity of ethnic and family background but by diversity of academic preparation.

claims.text: In nations with core curricula, such as Finland and France, the disparity in student readiness decreases over time.
claims.source_passage: In core-curriculum nations such as Finland and France, the disparity in student readiness decreases over time.

claims.text: Instituting a year-by-year common core curriculum is especially helpful for disadvantaged students who change schools frequently.
claims.source_passage: Instituting a core of year-by-year commonality among different schools to offer more coherent schooling is especially helpful for disadvantaged students who change schools.

claims.text: Specific grade-by-grade planning allows for the integration of history, art, and music into a single curriculum.
claims.source_passage: Specific, grade-by-grade planning allows the entire curriculum to be integrated. The history of a period can be integrated with its art and music.

claims.text: Many American teachers spend several weeks at the start of each year in a 'mini-course' review of previous knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Many American teachers say that they spend several weeks at the start of each year in review. That is, they offer a mini-course in the things students need to know to go forward.

claims.text: The repetitive review cycle in American schools contributes to rising teacher attrition rates by causing student boredom and confusion.
claims.source_passage: To students who already know those things, the review is an occasion to start shooting spitballs. Those who lack the needed knowledge are clueless and throw spit-balls, too. No wonder the attrition rate of teachers rises with each successive grade in American schools.

claims.text: Curricular integration leads to better student retention and fuller understanding.
claims.source_passage: Such integration leads to better retention and fuller understanding.

claims.text: Enabling all students to speak and write in standard forms of English is a highly democratic and egalitarian aim.
claims.source_passage: It is a highly democratic aim to enable everybody to speak and write in the standard forms, to ensure that one’s education, brains, or social class will not be disparaged.

claims.text: The educational task of schools is to adapt students to the standard language, rather than adapting the language to the student.
claims.source_passage: The task of the schools is to adapt the student to the language, not vice versa.

claims.text: The abandonment of grammar instruction by schools makes social mobility more difficult for students from non-educated homes.
claims.source_passage: The most unfortunate consequence of the schools’ abandonment of grammar is that social mobility becomes ever more difficult for those students who did not grow up in educated homes, for whom the difference between oral and written language is great.

claims.text: Grammar instruction is necessary to prevent students from being perceived as incompetent or unworthy of attention in the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: The purpose of grammar instruction is to give students the ability to write and speak confidently in the public sphere without committing the errors that will cause other Americans to think of them as incompetent or not worth listening to.

claims.text: Hypercorrection errors like 'between he and I' are direct results of the 'anti-grammar regime' in modern schooling.
claims.source_passage: Educated under the anti-curriculum, antigrammar regime, an astonishing number of well-read and intelligent Americans make this mistake. They speak in this unnatural, self-conscious way because grammar has not been taught well—if at all.

claims.text: To achieve conversancy in Standard English, the early curriculum should focus specifically on subject-verb number agreement and pronoun case.
claims.source_passage: To make students conversant with Standard English in early grades, the most important areas of grammar are subject-verb number agreement and pronoun case.

claims.text: Students often fear making grammatical mistakes because they have not been properly taught the specific points of grammar needed to avoid social scorn.
claims.source_passage: they are worried about making a mistake and because no one has properly taught them the very few points of grammar needed to avoid scorn.

claims.text: Subject-verb number agreement and pronoun case are the most important areas of grammar for making students conversant with Standard English in early grades.
claims.source_passage: To make students conversant with Standard English in early grades, the most important areas of grammar are subject-verb number agreement and pronoun case.

claims.text: Existing pronouns in Standard English are vestigial remains from a time when English used word forms (inflections) to indicate word relationships.
claims.source_passage: the existing pronouns of Standard English are leftover vestiges from a time when English used word forms rather than word order to indicate word relationships.

claims.text: Old English and Latin indicated grammatical relations by word form while keeping word order more or less the same.
claims.source_passage: in Old English (as in Latin) such relations could be shown by word form while keeping the word order more or less the same.

claims.text: Modern English pronouns still use word forms to indicate relationships, regardless of sentence position.
claims.source_passage: “I hit him “is not the same as “Me he hit”—quite the contrary—though I/me comes first in both sentences.

claims.text: Teaching students the simple rules of pronoun case is a great service that prevents them from having to guess in speech.
claims.source_passage: It is a very simple matter to teach students how to do this so they will never forget it. It will be a great service to them if we do.

claims.text: Knowledge of sentence structure, including phrases and clauses, is required to distinguish between grammatical subjects and objects.
claims.source_passage: to know the difference between I and me, she and her, one has to know the difference between a subject and an object, so it is important to understand what phrases and clauses are and to know something about sentence structure.

claims.text: It is necessary and convenient to teach young students a specific technical vocabulary for language, including parts of speech and grammatical categories.
claims.source_passage: To impart such knowledge it is convenient to teach young students a vocabulary about language—such words as subject and object, singular and plural, past, present, and future, sentence and clause, as well as the parts of speech: noun, verb, adjective, adverb, article, pronoun, conjunction, preposition.

claims.text: Educational research claiming that grammar instruction is harmful or useless is spurious and questionable.
claims.source_passage: American teachers are told that research shows such grammar instruction to be harmful or useless, but it is this spurious research that is questionable, not intelligent instruction in the grammar of the standard language.

claims.text: Intelligent instruction in the parts of speech is as harmless as teaching the parts of plants but more important for a child's future.
claims.source_passage: Teaching a few simple things about the parts of speech... is no more harmful than teaching the basic parts of plants. And for the child’s future it is enormously more important.

claims.text: Among education leaders, Albert Shanker was uniquely blunt in opposing the proposal to teach Ebonics in schools.
claims.source_passage: Al and I were the lone dissenters. I politely mentioned some technical objections. He simply said, 'It’s a terrible idea.' Among educators, only Al had the courage to be so blunt.

claims.text: The widespread adoption of natural reading methods resulted in hundreds of thousands of students becoming semiliterate before research by the National Institutes of Health debunked the theory.
claims.source_passage: During the past two decades the National Institutes of Health have exploded this idea through exhaustive research, but not before hundreds thousands of students had been consigned to semiliteracy.

claims.text: The honorable intent behind the 'Students’ Right to Their Own Language' resolution is that teachers should avoid making students feel that their home dialect is unworthy.
claims.source_passage: The honorable impulse behind the resolution is that teachers should not make students feel that their home speech is somehow unworthy. But that is all it should have said.

claims.text: Refusing to teach children the standard dialect in the name of avoiding oppression is itself an act of oppression, as it denies them the tools required for full societal participation.
claims.source_passage: By equating the teaching of a standard American dialect with oppression and by refusing to give students the linguistic tools they need to be full participants in society, we are in fact oppressing them.

claims.text: Sensible parents universally want their own children to achieve mastery of the standard language, regardless of abstract theoretical arguments for non-standard dialects.
claims.source_passage: The implication of her title is that while it’s all very well to pronounce that students have a right to their own language, sensible parents will certainly want their own children to wield the standard language effectively.

claims.text: When schools are not explicit in teaching the standard language, students perceive it as teachers keeping secrets and abdicating their professional duty.
claims.source_passage: If such explicitness is not provided to students, what it feels like to people who are old enough to judge is that there are secrets being kept, that time is being wasted, that the teacher is abdicating his or her duty to teach.

claims.text: Just as Thomas Jefferson separated religious worship from public duty, Americans can separate private dialects from the public necessity of using Standard English.
claims.source_passage: Just as Jefferson did not care what gods his neighbors might privately worship, he would not have cared what language or dialect they might speak at home. But whatever language Americans speak in the private sphere, they need to use Standard English in the public sphere.

claims.text: Academic theorists who oppose the common language are merely one faction within the public sphere, and they paradoxically use the very language they condemn to communicate their arguments.
claims.source_passage: Self-righteous ideologues who call the standard American language a mere class dialect are simply one more faction within the multifarious fabric of the whole, and like everyone else who commands attention in the public sphere, they conduct their agitations in the very language they condemn.

claims.text: The United States is uniquely the 'child of literacy and schooling' because it was founded specifically during the era when national language standardization emerged.
claims.source_passage: The United States was born in the era of language standardization and universal literacy. It is the very child of literacy and schooling.

claims.text: The United States military is the nation's most meritocratic institution, and it requires mastery of Standard English as a prerequisite for advancement.
claims.source_passage: Many of them came from humble origins and rose by merit within the military—our most meritocratic institution. All generals speak and write Standard English in the public sphere.

claims.text: If reading and writing were merely technical skills rather than knowledge-based activities, the education system could be reduced to only four years.
claims.source_passage: If reading and writing were simply how-to skills, three or four years would suffice. You could graduate after fourth grade.

claims.text: The long-term persistence of Blair's Rhetoric as a standard work at Harvard demonstrates that core public knowledge is durable rather than ephemeral.
claims.source_passage: In deciding on the core contents of the school curriculum, it makes sense, therefore, to place stress on the most durable, least ephemeral tacit public knowledge. That was the conscious purpose of Blair’s Rhetoric, and it explains why the Harvard Library holds 124 editions of that work from 1793 to 1990.

claims.text: The placement of a nominative pronoun at the end of a clause or sentence violates the fundamental word-order principles of modern English.
claims.source_passage: It goes against the word-order principle of modern English to put a nominative at the end of a clause or sentence.

claims.text: The NCTE assertion that the standard American dialect is a myth is the most ill-informed public statement ever made by a learned society.
claims.source_passage: the assertion that 'language scholars long ago denied that the myth of a standard American dialect has any validity,' the most ill-informed public statement by a learned society in many years.

claims.text: The mistake of the genetic fallacy is plausible in language study because standard language proficiency has become a practical prerequisite for membership in the socio-economic elite.
claims.source_passage: But to pay too much attention to dialectal origins is to commit the genetic fallacy... In the case of language in the modern world it is a plausible mistake, since you cannot belong to the top group these days unless you can read, write, and speak the standard language.

evidence.description: Exhaustive research by the National Institutes of Health over two decades that disproved the effectiveness of the whole-language method.
A verbatim reprint of a student essay from CCNY's English 110 which received an 'A' despite significant spelling and grammatical errors.
The experience of Al Fiellin at CCNY where faculty resisted a writing requirement because students' writing was beyond their help.
A published complaint from a beginning teacher regarding the lack of guidance on what grammar to teach.
Observation of remedial writing students at City’s General Education and Guidance Division losing skills due to lack of reinforcement.
The CCCC and NCTE resolution 'Students’ Right to Their Own Language' (1974/2003) which frames standard English as a myth of social dominance.
The presence of Standard English in newspapers, radio, and blogs as evidence of its reality.
Lisa Delpit's insights on the necessity of explicit instruction for empowering minority students.
Historical analysis of the American Founders' preference for linguistic standards to promote civility.
Historical account of the Founders' preference for common language as a means to push back 'barbarism.'
The similarity between Noah Webster's American dictionary and Samuel Johnson's English dictionary, showing continuity.
evidence.source_reference: Noah Webster's spelling book and dictionary; Dr. Johnson’s dictionary

evidence.description: The observation that modern dictionary updates focus primarily on vocabulary (DVDs, blogs) rather than grammatical changes.
Webster’s New International Dictionary (1934) remains an accurate guide to language 75 years later, despite missing modern coinages.
Alexander Pope’s 1711 poem expresses the contemporary fear that English was changing so fast that 'Modern Rhymes' would be unreadable within sixty years.
Modern American schoolchildren can still read and understand the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution despite them being over 200 years old.
The Old English legal code (text cited) is completely unintelligible to modern English speakers without specialized study.
Linguistic mapping by Angus Macintosh showed that premodern British manuscripts could be pinpointed to within 20 miles based on distinct local dialects.
The speech patterns of US Army generals, many from humble origins, who must speak Standard English to achieve their rank, regardless of their social class background.
The experience of reading British newspapers online, which reveals that understanding requires specific unstated knowledge unique to the British public sphere.
Scientific research on the effectiveness of systematic alphabetic instruction, including phonemic awareness and blending.
evidence.source_reference: National Reading Panel; National Institutes of Health

evidence.description: NAEP reading score data from 1971 to 2004 for ages 9, 13, and 17, showing stagnant performance in older students despite early-grade gains.
NAEP student reading scores from 1971-2004 for ages nine, thirteen, and seventeen, showing stagnating or declining performance trends despite educational interventions.
The Nixon Watergate Tapes, which were incomprehensible to scholars until extensive context was provided, illustrating how much meaning is assumed in speech.
evidence.source_reference: Transcripts of the Nixon Watergate tapes

evidence.description: A linguistic thought experiment regarding a baseball game summary ('Sampson sacrificed and knocked in a run'), showing how Americans can construct a complex 'situation model' that Britons cannot.
The opening sentence of Pride and Prejudice, used to demonstrate that failing to understand irony is often a result of lacking background knowledge rather than lacking skill.
NAEP data shows that thirteen years of schooling in the US has not sufficed for mature literacy.
Blair’s Rhetoric had 124 editions in the Harvard Library between 1793 and 1990, demonstrating its role in standardizing public knowledge.
Stevenson and Stigler found that academic preparation diversity, not ethnic diversity, hinders classroom productivity.
evidence.source_reference: Harold Stevenson and James Stigler, The Learning Gap

evidence.description: Comparative observation of Finland and France showing decreased student disparity in core-curriculum nations.
Data showing 70 percent of low-income students change schools by third grade.
Statistics on the school mobility of low-income students by the third grade.
evidence.source_reference: Not specified in text (likely later endnote)

evidence.description: Historical account of the egalitarian goals of American schooling regarding language.
NCTE resolution claiming isolated grammar exercises deter improvement.
evidence.source_reference: National Council of Teachers of English

evidence.description: The linguistic observation that standard written English forms are historically stable and conservative.
The semantic difference between 'Dog bites man' and 'Man bites dog' illustrates how word order determines meaning in modern English.
Comparison of 'I hit him' and 'Me he hit' shows that pronoun form (case) overrides word order in determining grammatical role.
The author contrasts modern English with the grammatical structures of Latin and Old English to explain the origin of pronoun case.

concepts.author_definition: An instructional approach based on the theory that immersion in authentic language experiences allows students to decode symbols into sounds naturally without the 'artificial' study of phonics.

The educational belief that language acquisition is entirely natural and that teachers should not worry about teaching correct pronunciation or grammar because it will 'work out for the best' without intervention.
The specific linguistic code used in the American public sphere and academic writing that teachers are currently failing to transmit to students.

concepts.term: Students’ Right to Their Own Language
concepts.author_definition: A 1974 resolution by CCCC/NCTE that Hirsch characterizes as a well-intentioned but 'muddled' and 'factually inadmissible' ideology that harms students by withholding standard linguistic tools.
concepts.source_passage: In the early 1970s... the Conference on College Composition and Communication and the National Council of Teachers of English, put out a well-intentioned but muddled resolution called Students’ Right to Their Own Language.

concepts.author_definition: The distinction where private dialects/languages are respected (private sphere) but Standard English is mandatory for shared democratic and economic life (public sphere).
concepts.source_passage: But whatever language Americans speak in the private sphere, they need to use Standard English in the public sphere

concepts.author_definition: The logical error of assuming that the origins or 'genesis' of a thing (like a language) permanently define and limit its current nature or social function.
A shared public language (Standard English) that belongs to no specific subgroup but serves as the universal medium for democratic participation.
concepts.common_alternative: A class dialect used by the elite to exclude others.

concepts.author_definition: An artificial, stable construct created over centuries through dictionaries, grammar books, and schools to provide a common print and speech code.

The process, beginning with the printing press and accelerated by national academies and universal schooling, of establishing uniform syntax, grammar, spelling, and pronunciation across a large geographical area.
The standardized version of a language used in writing and formal education, which eventually stabilizes and becomes the public sphere's common tongue.
The phenomenon where a language becomes resistant to change in its core grammar and syntax once its standard forms are codified in dictionaries and taught in schools.
The shared background information and values taken for granted by writers communicating with a general audience; it is the essential ingredient for language comprehension beyond mechanics.
A mental representation of the state of affairs described in a text, constructed by combining verbal cues with background knowledge to make inferences.
The implicitly standardized body of shared information that accompanies standard language in the public sphere.
A group of people who share not only a language but also the unspoken background knowledge and attitudes required for full membership and communication.
The specific body of knowledge all children need to know to flourish in American society, as defined by Carleton Washburne.
The dominant educational establishment that opposes any specific, pre-set grade-by-grade curriculum.
An error occurring when a speaker, in an attempt to be very correct, uses a grammatical form (like the nominative case) where it does not belong (like the objective case).
The current educational framework advocated by organizations like the NCTE that discourages direct instruction in grammar and specific subject matter.
A language, like modern English, where the relationships between words are indicated primarily by their position in the sentence.
A language, like Old English or Latin, where relationships between words are indicated by word forms or endings rather than position.
Leftover grammatical forms (like 'me' or 'whom') from an earlier inflected stage of the English language that remain in the modern standard language despite it being primarily word-order based.
The author's characterization of pedagogical research that claims teaching formal grammar to children is ineffective or detrimental.
A group that shares the unspoken background knowledge and cultural attitudes required to comprehend irony and nuance in literary texts.
The structural rule of modern English where grammatical relations are indicated by the position of words in a sentence rather than by their inflected forms.
A student's perception of educational neglect that occurs when a teacher fails to explicitly teach the conventions and knowledge of the dominant public language.

cases.name: New England Primer of the 1680s
cases.description: The first textbook of the American colonies which used religious and moral lessons (e.g., 'In Adam's fall, we sin-ned all') to teach the alphabet, illustrating that textbooks have historically sought to impart values alongside literacy.
key_passages: The first textbook of the American colonies, the Puritans’ New England Primer of the 1680s, did not say, “A is for Apple.” It said, “In Adam’s fall, we sin-ned all.”

cases.name: CCNY English 110 Writing Standards
cases.description: An example of a nonremedial college course where a student essay with extreme phonetic spelling and grammatical errors received an 'A', demonstrating the collapse of writing standards in higher education.
key_passages: The following short essay—reprinted here verbatim—received an A in [CCNY’s] English 110, a nonremedial course... 'I would tell Charles to read and then don’t just stop and go do some thing else but to rectie...'

cases.name: The American Founding Era
cases.description: A period where language standardization was intentionally pursued through dictionaries and education to foster civility and cosmopolitanism among citizens of the new nation.
key_passages: Gordon S. Wood reminds us that the American Founders preferred a common language not just for practical reasons but also to support ideals of civility, mutual respect, and goodwill
Compilers of dictionaries attempted to find the correct meanings, spellings, and punctuations of words, and freeze them between the covers of their books.

cases.name: Premodern Britain
cases.description: A period characterized by extreme linguistic fragmentation where local dialects varied so significantly over small distances (20–100 miles) that they were often mutually unintelligible, and legal codes are today unrecognizable as English.
key_passages: Before mass literacy, local dialects were so distinct that the historical linguist Angus Macintosh used to say he could identify within twenty miles where a premodern British manuscript had been written.
Britons’ earlier legal code is simply unintelligible to all but scholars of Old English

cases.name: The Founding of the United States
cases.description: The U.S. was established at the exact historical moment when English language standardization had stabilized, allowing its foundational legal documents to remain intelligible to citizens centuries later.
key_passages: The United States was born in the era of language standardization and universal literacy. It is the very child of literacy and schooling.
Our founding documents, the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution, are written and spelled in essentially our own language

cases.name: The Nixon Watergate Tapes
cases.description: Secretly recorded Oval Office conversations that required massive scholarly annotation to be understood by the public because the participants (Nixon, Shultz, etc.) took so much specific knowledge for granted.
key_passages: When President Richard Nixon taped his Oval Office conversations, he left behind a trove of linguistic evidence showing how much is silently assumed in the speech of small groups.
Transcripts of the Nixon Watergate tapes were in many places simply incomprehensible until scholars surrounded the text with a lot of this necessary information.

cases.name: Winnetka, Illinois Schools (1919-1943)
cases.description: Under Superintendent Carleton Washburne, the district framed its curriculum around 'common essentials'—knowledge required for children to flourish. This approach was resisted by progressives who opposed pre-set curricula.
key_passages: Carleton Washburne, superintendent of schools in Winnetka, Illinois, from 1919 to 1943. He asked what things all children need to know to flourish in American society today and then framed the curriculum around these “common essentials.”

cases.name: Core Knowledge Schools
cases.description: Hundreds of schools following the Core Knowledge Sequence, a coherent grade-by-grade core curriculum based on the index Hirsch developed in 1987. These schools reportedly show significant gains in reading comprehension.
key_passages: In the two decades since then, my colleagues and I at the Core Knowledge Foundation have transformed that list into a coherent core curriculum that is now being followed by hundreds of schools. Unsurprisingly, reading comprehension scores at these schools have soared.

cases.name: The Traditional Grammar School
cases.description: A historical model of the common school where teaching grammar—including sentence diagramming and definitions of parts of speech—was central to the curriculum to prepare students for formal written language.
key_passages: In the past, the teaching of grammar was a central feature of the common school (hence the nearly obsolete phrase “grammar school”). The curriculum was designed to familiarize students with the norms of formal written language. They had to diagram sentences to show they understood syntactical relationships.

thinkers.name: Albert Shanker
thinkers.key_idea: Teaching 'Ebonics' in schools is a 'terrible idea' and schools should focus on the standard code.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Shanker's bluntness approvingly as a rare instance of an educator standing against linguistic 'imperialism' charges to support standard instruction.
key_passages: Al and I were the lone dissenters. I politely mentioned some technical objections. He simply said, “It’s a terrible idea.” Among educators, only Al had the courage to be so blunt. I will try to explain why he was right

thinkers.name: Heather Macdonald
thinkers.key_idea: Writing standards in universities like CCNY have declined to the point where failing work receives high grades.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Macdonald's reporting as empirical evidence for the consequences of the naturalistic movement's failure to teach grammar and writing.
key_passages: Heather Macdonald gives an example in City Journal: The following short essay—reprinted here verbatim—received an A in [CCNY’s] English 110

thinkers.name: Al Fiellin
thinkers.key_idea: Remedial students lose writing skills when those skills are not required across the core curriculum.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Fiellin's experience at City’s General Education and Guidance Division to demonstrate the failure of isolated remediation.
key_passages: Al Fiellin... discovered that remedial writing students were losing through disuse whatever skills they had acquired in remediation.

thinkers.name: Antonio Gramsci
thinkers.key_idea: Mastery of the 'dialect of power' is necessary for those who seek to challenge or access power.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch invokes Gramsci to argue that withholding standard English from students is a form of oppression rather than liberation.
key_passages: Even if one argues (incorrectly) that there is no standard American dialect but only a dialect of “power,” to restrict students’ mastery of that dialect is to deny them access to power, as Gramsci and others after him have observed.

thinkers.name: Lisa Delpit
thinkers.key_idea: Teachers have a duty to explicitly teach the conventions of the standard language to 'other people's children' to avoid 'keeping secrets' from them.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Delpit as a 'powerful' source of insight regarding the school's duty to empower minority students with the standard language.
key_passages: As an African-American educator, Delpit is sensitive to the schools’ duty to empower students with the standard language... Delpit says, 'If such explicitness is not provided to students... there are secrets being kept... the teacher is abdicating his or her duty to teach.'

thinkers.name: Gordon S. Wood
thinkers.key_idea: The American Founders sought linguistic standards to promote civility, cosmopolitanism, and a liberal arts ideal.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Wood's historical scholarship to ground his own argument for a common language in the civic ideals of the American founding.
key_passages: Gordon S. Wood reminds us that the American Founders preferred a common language not just for practical reasons but also to support ideals of civility, mutual respect, and goodwill

thinkers.name: Noah Webster
thinkers.key_idea: Standardized American spelling and vocabulary based heavily on English precedents.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Webster to show that the American language was not a new class dialect but an inherited standardization.
key_passages: Noah Webster’s spelling book and dictionary offered but slight deviations from Dr. Johnson’s dictionary.

thinkers.name: Alexander Pope
thinkers.key_idea: The belief that language change is so rapid that writers' works become unreadable within 60 years (a 'threescore' lifespan for fame).
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Pope to illustrate how language was perceived just before standardization took hold, noting that Pope was 'lucky' to flourish right when the stability he feared lost was actually beginning.
key_passages: In the early eighteenth century, just before language standardization became dominant in England, Alexander Pope worried that he would not be understood after sixty years

thinkers.name: Henry Bradley
thinkers.key_idea: Predicted in 1904 that English grammar would see few changes after the nineteenth century.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch credits Bradley for correctly predicting the stability of standardized languages, though Hirsch suggests the stability actually dates back even further to the eighteenth century.
key_passages: The linguist Henry Bradley... deserves credit for first predicting the stability of the newly standardized languages.

thinkers.name: Angus Macintosh
thinkers.key_idea: Could identify the geographical origin of premodern British manuscripts within 20 miles based on dialect.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Macintosh's expertise to prove how fragmented and localized language was before mass literacy and standardization.
key_passages: the historical linguist Angus Macintosh used to say he could identify within twenty miles where a premodern British manuscript had been written.

thinkers.name: George Bernard Shaw
thinkers.key_idea: The United States and Britain are 'two nations separated by a common language.'
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch argues that Shaw's observation is correct but misattributed; the separation isn't about grammar or spelling, but about the different pools of tacit knowledge assumed in each nation's public sphere.
key_passages: While George Bernard Shaw’s wisecrack about the two nations being separated by a common language has an element of truth, it has almost nothing to do with grammar and spelling and everything to do with tacit knowledge.

thinkers.name: Jane Austen
thinkers.key_idea: The use of irony in social commentary, as exemplified in the opening of Pride and Prejudice.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Austen's irony to argue that comprehension of sophisticated literary devices depends on specific cultural and linguistic knowledge rather than just abstract 'critical thinking' skills.
key_passages: If young readers don’t comprehend Jane Austen’s irony, it would not be because they lacked humor or subtlety but because they lacked knowledge.

thinkers.name: Carleton Washburne
thinkers.key_idea: Schools should teach 'common essentials'—specific knowledge required for flourishing in society.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch aligns himself with Washburne's curricular approach and notes that Washburne was unfairly criticized by progressives for it.
key_passages: Washburne met with disapproval from fellow progressives, who opposed any specific curriculum set in advance. In his innocence, he did not realize that he had transgressed the anti-curriculum principle.

thinkers.name: Harold Stevenson and James Stigler
thinkers.key_idea: Diversity of academic preparation, not ethnic background, is the root cause of low classroom productivity in the US.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites their findings to support his argument that a common curriculum (which standardizes preparation) is the solution to educational inefficiency.
key_passages: As Harold Stevenson and James Stigler pointed out in their pathbreaking book The Learning Gap, the American classroom’s lack of productivity is not chiefly caused by diversity of ethnic and family background but by diversity of academic preparation.

actors.name: National Institutes of Health (NIH)
actors.context: Conducted exhaustive research that 'exploded' the whole-language theory.

actors.name: Al Fiellin
actors.context: Former dean at CCNY who attempted to introduce writing requirements but met faculty resistance due to student incompetence.

actors.name: Anti-curriculum movement
actors.context: Promoted natural methods of teaching reading and attacked grammar instruction.

actors.name: Conference on College Composition and Communication (CCCC)
actors.context: Professional organization that authored the 'Students’ Right to Their Own Language' resolution.

actors.name: National Council of Teachers of English (NCTE)
actors.context: Professional organization that reaffirmed the 1974 resolution in 2003.

actors.name: Academic Theorists
actors.context: Those who claim Standard English is merely a tool of the white, Anglo-Saxon bourgeoisie.

actors.name: Samuel Johnson
actors.context: English dictionary compiler who provided the basis for American language standardization.

actors.name: Dictionary compilers
actors.context: They emphasize small vocabulary changes while glossing over the massive stability of the core language.

actors.name: National Academies
actors.context: Institutions in countries like Britain, France, Germany, and Spain that helped self-consciously standardize national languages.

actors.name: National Council of Teachers of English (NCTE)
actors.context: Associated with the view that Standard English is primarily a marker of social class.

actors.name: National Reading Panel
actors.context: Publicized scientific work on the systematic teaching of alphabetic reading.

actors.name: National Institutes of Health (NIH)
actors.context: Sponsored scientific work on phonics and the mechanics of literacy.

actors.name: National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP)
actors.context: Source of longitudinal reading score data showing trends from 1971 to 2004.

actors.name: Richard Nixon
actors.context: His secret White House recordings serve as evidence for the role of unstated knowledge in communication.

actors.name: George P. Shultz
actors.context: Mentioned as Nixon's budget director in the context of the Watergate tape transcripts.

actors.name: Core Knowledge Foundation
actors.context: The organization that developed and promotes the Core Knowledge curriculum based on Hirsch's work.

actors.name: Progressives
actors.context: Educational theorists who oppose a specific, pre-set core curriculum.

actors.name: National Council of Teachers of English (NCTE)
actors.context: Advocates for the 'Students’ Right to Their Own Language' and resolutions against isolated grammar instruction.

actors.name: Schools of Education
actors.context: Cited as the source of 'bad advice' regarding language-arts instruction.

actors.name: American teachers
actors.context: The group being told by educational researchers that grammar instruction is harmful.

actors.name: Educational Researchers
actors.context: The unnamed authors of studies claiming grammar instruction is useless, which Hirsch labels as 'spurious'.

objections_raised.objection: Teaching standard English to students who speak different dialects at home is a form of language imperialism.
objections_raised.response: The author argues that teaching the common language is a necessary goal of the common school to allow all citizens to participate in the public sphere, as supported by Albert Shanker's position.

objections_raised.objection: Teaching formal grammar is ineffective for developing English skills.
objections_raised.response: The author suggests this view is based on a narrow field of research and that the lack of grammar instruction has actually led to the current crisis in student writing.

objections_raised.objection: The claim that any one dialect is standard or 'acceptable' is an attempt by one social group to exert dominance over another and is 'immoral' advice.
objections_raised.response: Refusing to teach the standard dialect is the true form of oppression, as it denies students the economic and civic 'tools' they need to challenge that dominance or participate in society.

objections_raised.objection: The standard language is an imperialistic imposition of the white, Anglo-Saxon bourgeoisie meant to marginalize other dialects.
objections_raised.response: This is a genetic fallacy; origins do not define current function. Furthermore, the theorists themselves use the standard language to make their case because it is the only way to gain attention in the public sphere.

objections_raised.objection: Language is constantly and rapidly changing, as evidenced by the new words appearing in dictionaries every decade.
objections_raised.response: Vocabulary changes (like DVD or blog) represent only a tiny segment of the language; the core grammar, syntax, and spelling are extremely conservative and have changed very little for over a century.

objections_raised.objection: Standard English is merely a marker of social class, and emphasizing it reinforces class hierarchies.
objections_raised.source: National Council of Teachers of English
objections_raised.response: The association with class is contingent. Standard English is actually a class-transcending tool and a prerequisite for meritocratic mobility, as evidenced by successful individuals from humble origins (like army generals).

objections_raised.objection: Reading comprehension is a formal, transferable ability that can be learned through practicing strategy drills.
objections_raised.response: This is a 'tragic mistake' because comprehension is content-dependent and requires the specific background knowledge assumed by a text.

objections_raised.objection: Specifying a curriculum in advance is inappropriate or restrictive.
objections_raised.response: The author argues that without a specific grade-by-grade curriculum, academic disparity increases, teacher attrition rises, and disadvantaged students are harmed by lack of coherence.

objections_raised.objection: Isolated grammar and usage exercises not supported by theory and research are a deterrent to the improvement of students’ speaking and writing.
objections_raised.response: The author characterizes this as 'bad advice' from 'learned societies' that have departed from common sense, resulting in 'hypercorrection' and reduced social mobility.

objections_raised.objection: Research shows that formal grammar instruction is harmful or useless for students' writing and development.
objections_raised.source: Unnamed educational researchers (as cited by Hirsch)
objections_raised.response: The research itself is spurious and questionable; limited, intelligent instruction in the parts of speech is harmless and essential for the child's future mobility.
